1: A revelation of God: YHWH speaks to Moses from within the tent of meeting — the completed tabernacle has become the place of divine speech. The God who commands sacrifice is the God who will himself provide the ultimate sacrifice; the instructions now given from within the sanctuary point forward to the one who will enter the greater sanctuary not made with hands (Hebrews 9:11).
2: A shadow: 'Speak to the people of Israel and say to them, When any one of you brings an offering to YHWH, you shall bring your offering of livestock from the herd or from the flock.' The entire Levitical offering system is a comprehensive shadow-structure. Hebrews calls the law 'a shadow of the good things to come' (Hebrews 10:1); what is outlined in exhaustive detail in Leviticus 1–7 is the shadow of the one sacrifice of Christ.
3: A direct type: 'If his offering is a burnt offering from the herd, he shall offer a male without blemish [tamim].' The requirement of tamim (complete, without defect) in the sacrificial animal is the OT's most persistent type of Christ's sinlessness. 1 Peter 1:18–19 makes the connection explicit: 'You were ransomed... with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish [amōmos] or spot.' Hebrews 9:14 similarly: 'Christ... offered himself without blemish to God.' Every animal rejected for defect announced that only a perfect substitute could make atonement.
4: A direct type: 'He shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering, and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him.' The hand-laying rite (semichah) is the sacrificial system's most explicit act of identification and substitution — the offerer transfers his own identity onto the animal, which then stands in his place. The NT applies this substitutionary logic to Christ: 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us' (2 Corinthians 5:21); 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree' (1 Peter 2:24). The hand-press of semichah becomes the imputation of the believer's sin to Christ.
5: A direct type: 'Then he shall kill the bull before YHWH, and Aaron's sons the priests shall bring the blood and throw the blood against the sides of the altar.' Blood thrown against the altar is the essential sacrificial act — Hebrews 9:22: 'Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.' The requirement that blood be actively presented (not merely spilled) reflects the deliberate, purposeful nature of atonement — and points to Christ's deliberate, voluntary death (John 10:17–18: 'I lay it down of my own accord').
6: A shadow: 'And he shall flay the burnt offering and cut it into pieces.' The complete dismemberment and examination of the offering ensures that nothing is concealed or hidden — the sacrifice must be transparent, without hidden defects. Christ submitted to complete exposure: interrogated, stripped, examined publicly — 'this is the judgment, that the light has come into the world' (John 3:19).
7: A shadow: 'The sons of Aaron the priest shall put fire on the altar and arrange wood on the fire.' The altar fire was kept continuously burning (Leviticus 6:12–13, 'the fire on the altar shall be kept burning; it shall not go out'). This perpetual fire is the image of God's consuming holiness — the fire into which Christ entered: 'our God is a consuming fire' (Hebrews 12:29). The cross was not an accident of history but a divine altar-fire prepared before creation (Revelation 13:8).
8: A shadow: 'And Aaron's sons the priests shall arrange the pieces, the head, and the fat, on the wood that is on the fire on the altar.' Every part of the sacrifice is carefully arranged — nothing is left off the altar. Christ's offering was total: body, soul, and spirit fully given. 'I give my flesh for the life of the world' (John 6:51) — and more than flesh: 'He offered himself without blemish to God' (Hebrews 9:14).
9: A direct type: 'Its entrails and its legs he shall wash with water. And the priest shall burn all of it on the altar, as a burnt offering, a food offering with a pleasing aroma [reah nichoach] to YHWH.' The olah (burnt offering) is entirely consumed — the word means 'ascending'; every part rises to God as smoke. This total consumption is the type of Christ's total self-giving: 'He loved me and gave himself for me' (Galatians 2:20). Paul describes Christ's sacrifice using the reah nichoach formula exactly: 'a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Ephesians 5:2).
10: A direct type: 'If his gift for a burnt offering is from the flock, from the sheep or goats, he shall bring a male without blemish [tamim].' The requirement of perfection is repeated for each animal category. The repetition is pedagogical — Israel is trained through constant repetition to understand that no imperfect substitute can stand. Christ's sinlessness is not incidental but structurally required by the entire sacrificial system: 'a lamb without blemish or spot, foreknown before the foundation of the world' (1 Peter 1:19–20).
11: A shadow: 'And he shall kill it on the north side of the altar before YHWH.' The north side of the altar became the traditional slaughter site — the geography of sacrifice is defined. At Golgotha, the site of Christ's death was outside the north gate of Jerusalem, outside the camp (Hebrews 13:12) — matching the pattern that sin-offerings were burned outside the camp (Leviticus 4:12, 21).
12: A shadow: 'And he shall cut it into pieces, with its head and its fat, and the priest shall arrange them on the wood that is on the fire on the altar.' The care taken in the arrangement of every piece emphasizes completeness — no part left off. Christ's atonement is complete: 'It is finished' (John 19:30) — the Greek tetelestai is an accounting term for 'paid in full.'
13: A direct type: 'But the entrails and the legs he shall wash with water. And the priest shall offer all of it and burn it on the altar; it is a burnt offering, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The repeated reah nichoach formula (Leviticus 1:9, 13, 17) hammers the note of divine acceptance. The cross was not defeat but acceptance — the Father accepted the sacrifice: 'Therefore God has highly exalted him' (Philippians 2:9). Paul explicitly applies the Levitical formula: Christ 'gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Ephesians 5:2).
14: A direct type: 'If his offering to YHWH is a burnt offering of birds, he shall bring his offering of turtledoves or pigeons.' The bird offering was the poor man's provision — the same offering Mary brought at Jesus's presentation (Luke 2:24: 'a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons'), identifying Christ with the poor. James 2:5: 'Has not God chosen those who are poor in the world to be rich in faith?' Christ entered the sacrificial system from the lowest economic level of offering, signaling that atonement is accessible regardless of wealth.
15: A shadow: 'And the priest shall bring it to the altar and wring off its head and burn it on the altar. Its blood shall be drained out on the side of the altar.' The bird offering involves the priest's direct action — he himself wrings off the head. Christ's sacrifice involved direct divine action: 'It was the will of YHWH to crush him' (Isaiah 53:10). The Spirit came upon Jesus as a dove at his baptism (Matthew 3:16) — marking him as the dove-offering that the Father himself would offer.
16: A shadow: 'He shall remove its crop with its contents and cast it beside the altar on the east side, in the place for ashes.' The crop is removed — what the bird has ingested, what it has fed on from the earth. Christ, though taking on human flesh, was without sin — 'holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners' (Hebrews 7:26). The removal of the crop is a type of Christ's complete freedom from what corrupts.
17: A direct type: 'He shall tear it open by its wings, but shall not sever it completely. And the priest shall burn it on the altar, on the wood that is on the fire. It is a burnt offering, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The third repetition of reah nichoach closes chapter 1. The threefold repetition — for cattle (v9), small cattle (v13), birds (v17) — spans all economic levels of offering. At every level, the same result: a pleasing aroma accepted by God. The richest and the poorest brought the same acceptance. Christ, offered once, was accepted for all (Hebrews 10:14: 'by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified').
